title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about the development of materials science and its impact on everyday life. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Invisible Revolution
content.subtitle: A science magazine article about how advances in materials science are quietly reshaping the world.
content.text: Walk through any modern city and you are surrounded by miracles you have learned to ignore. The glass in your office building filters ultraviolet light and adjusts its own tint in response to sunlight. The concrete beneath your feet contains fibres that prevent it from cracking under load. The coat you wore this morning repels water without any visible coating, thanks to a surface texture engineered at the scale of nanometres. [31] We live in what materials scientists sometimes call 'the age of designed matter' — an era in which the properties of physical substances are no longer simply discovered but deliberately constructed. [/31] || The discipline of materials science is relatively young as a formal field, having only emerged as a distinct area of study in the 1950s. But its roots reach back to the earliest humans who discovered that clay hardened in fire, that copper could be alloyed with tin, that glass emerged from sand at extreme heat. [32] What distinguishes modern materials science from these ancient practices is not the basic ambition — to make matter do new things — but the precision with which that ambition can now be pursued. [/32] || Professor Amara Singh, who leads the Advanced Materials Laboratory at the University of Bristol, describes her work as 'engineering from the inside out'. Her team is currently developing a new class of polymer that can repair itself when damaged — an ability borrowed from the biological world. [33] 'Living organisms have always done this,' she explained. 'A cut in skin seals itself. A broken bone, given time, knits back together. We've spent decades trying to copy mechanisms that evolution perfected over millions of years.' [/33] || The applications of self-healing materials are potentially vast. Micro-cracks in aircraft components, bridges, and pipelines — damage that is currently difficult to detect and expensive to repair — could in principle be addressed automatically before they develop into serious structural failures. [34] The challenge, Professor Singh acknowledges, is not scientific but economic. The materials themselves can already be produced; the problem is producing them cheaply enough to be commercially viable. [/34] || Perhaps the most dramatic frontier in materials science is the development of so-called 'smart materials' — substances that change their properties in response to external stimuli such as temperature, light, or electrical current. Shape-memory alloys, for instance, can be deformed and will then return to their original form when heated. These are already used in medical stents and in the frames of some spectacles. [35] But researchers believe applications currently in development will make these uses look modest by comparison. [/35] || There is also a growing recognition within the field that the properties scientists design into new materials must be matched by equal care about what happens to those materials at the end of their useful life. [36] The development of biodegradable electronics — devices designed to dissolve harmlessly in soil after their working life — is one response to this challenge, and one that Professor Singh describes as 'the most important frontier of the next decade'. [/36]

questions.question: The phrase 'the age of designed matter' is used to highlight that
options: A) modern cities are filled with more technology than ever before.
B) scientists can now intentionally create specific material properties.
C) people no longer pay attention to the technology around them.
D) the pace of scientific change has made materials difficult to understand.
questions.explanation: The phrase contrasts 'discovered' with 'deliberately constructed', indicating that materials science is now able to engineer properties intentionally rather than finding them by chance.

questions.question: How does modern materials science differ from ancient practices according to the writer?
options: A) It involves a completely different goal — to create new substances rather than improve old ones.
B) It works with a far greater range of raw materials than ancient craftspeople had access to.
C) It is able to achieve the same fundamental aim with far greater accuracy.
D) It relies entirely on theoretical knowledge rather than practical experiment.
questions.explanation: The writer says the distinction is 'not the basic ambition' but 'the precision with which that ambition can now be pursued', indicating the goal is the same but the methods are more exact.

questions.question: Professor Singh refers to skin and bone in order to
options: A) argue that biological systems are still far superior to engineered ones.
B) illustrate the natural source of inspiration for self-healing polymer research.
C) suggest that the medical use of new materials is the most important application.
D) explain the long history of borrowing ideas from nature in materials science.
questions.explanation: Professor Singh uses skin and bone as examples of the biological self-repair mechanisms her team is trying to replicate in synthetic polymers — they are the source model, not a comparison or critique.

questions.question: What is currently preventing wider use of self-healing materials?
options: A) Researchers have not yet been able to produce them reliably in a laboratory.
B) The materials are too heavy to be practical in aerospace and construction.
C) The technology exists but the cost of producing it at scale remains too high.
D) There is insufficient demand from the construction and aerospace industries.
questions.explanation: Professor Singh explicitly states 'the materials themselves can already be produced; the problem is producing them cheaply enough to be commercially viable' — a production cost barrier.

questions.question: The writer mentions shape-memory alloys to show that
options: A) smart materials are already in use, though their current applications are limited.
B) the medical industry was the first to benefit from advances in smart materials.
C) the most important smart material applications are in the healthcare sector.
D) smart materials have been in commercial use for longer than most people realise.
questions.explanation: The writer uses shape-memory alloys as an example of smart materials already deployed, then adds that researchers believe future uses will 'make these uses look modest' — confirming current applications are present but limited.

questions.question: Professor Singh considers biodegradable electronics to be significant because
options: A) they represent the fastest-growing commercial sector in materials science.
B) they address the environmental problem of what happens to materials after use.
C) they are the first smart materials to be successfully produced at commercial scale.
D) they demonstrate how ancient techniques can be combined with modern science.
questions.explanation: The context makes clear that biodegradable electronics respond to the need to consider 'what happens to those materials at the end of their useful life' — an environmental end-of-life concern.
